contents.text: Test article text. There is lots of thext. Loriem ipsum sit dolor foobar blah blah
The assembler enforces a particular pattern of items in the inventory below it. This can be used to automatically fill out recipes for crafting or smelting. It requires a vascular network to run.

In the GUI, there are four blocks of slots. The first block is a read-only view of the inventory below, the second defines the desired pattern, the third contains recipe resources and the fourth is auxiliary output. Placing items into the green slots creates a pattern that the Assembler will try to replicate using items in the blue slots.

Clicking any red or green slot with 'Select Output Slots' enabled will cause the Assembler to extract recipe products from it. Assigning an item to the corresponding pattern slot, as shown above, will ensure that only the correct recipe product will be removed.
For crafting, the Persistent Crafting Table must be used, as a regular Crafting Table does not retain its items.
Instructions consist of an operation and a series of arguments. These arguments are positions in the world.
Instructions can have a variable number of arguments. Some, such as END and RESTART have zero arguments.

Certain arguments have to meet specific criteria, such as being item or fluid inventories. For example, COMBINE takes one item storage and one item workbench. If an invalid block is selected, the argument will be rejected.
NEEPASM is an assembly language for the NEEP PLC. Due to the PLC's Enlightened instruction set, the language can be slightly more user-friendly than most assembly languages.

Generally, programs are lists of instructions that are executed one after the other.
Instructions can take a variety of arguments, such as: 

- Strings: "a string"
- Integers: 123, 0x7B, 0b1111011
- World targets: @(<x>, <y>, <z>, <direction>).
World targets represent a block in the world and are used to tell instructions where to extract or insert fluids or items.
Anything occurring after a '#' character on a line is treated as a comment:
Flow control is accomplished by jumping between labels:
Commonly used groups of instructions can be defined in functions and world targets can be named using aliases:

The PLC has a stack for storing integers. Elements can be added and removed only from the top of the stack using the PUSH and POP instructions respectively.
The comparison and arithmetic instructions take their arguments directly from the stack:
add     # Add the last two entries
inc     # Increment the last stack entry

Labels are permanent marks in the program that can be returned to using the jump instructions: JMP, BIF and BIT.
For example, the following proram enters an infinite loop:
Labels can be reused. Adding 'f' or 'b' to the end of a branch instruction tries to find the label by looking forwards or backwards respectively:
Functions encapsulate procedures that can be executed multiple times.
A function is declared by surrounding instructions with %func and %end directives:
Aliases can be used to assign names to certain world targets. Aliases are recalled by prefixing their name with '$':
